nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated, non-migratory; commonly observed on clear coastal to outer reef slopes and drop-offs, typically below 15 m, often associated with staghorn coral thickets

decorPreferences: fine sand or crushed coral substrate

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding on small benthic invertebrates, coral polyps, and algae. In aquaria, generally accepts a varied diet of mysid shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, chopped krill, marine flake and pellet foods, and prepared frozen formulas containing marine algae and spirulina. Small portions offered several times daily tend to support better acclimation and long-term condition.

summary: A peaceful Western Pacific butterflyfish with a pearly white body, fine black reticulated 'crosshatch' pattern, and a contrasting orange-and-yellow tail. Generally considered not reef-safe due to coral polyp nipping; best kept in a peaceful FOWLR system.
careNotes:
The Pearlscale Butterflyfish (Chaetodon xanthurus) is generally considered an intermediate-level saltwater fish. It tends to be docile and peaceful and is reported to do well singly or as a bonded pair when both individuals are introduced simultaneously. Like many butterflyfishes, it has been reported to form monogamous pair bonds in the wild, so established pairs are generally advised not to be split.

This species is commonly considered not reef-safe — in the wild it has been documented feeding on hard and soft coral polyps and small benthic invertebrates, and is therefore best housed in a fish-only or FOWLR aquascape with plenty of live rock arranged into caves and overhangs plus generous open swimming space. Ornamental anemones, zoanthids, tubeworms, and clams are generally advised against.

New arrivals are often shy and have been reported to need extra care during acclimation; offering small amounts of varied meaty and algae-based foods several times daily tends to help them settle in.

A close lookalike, the Atoll Butterflyfish (Chaetodon mertensii), is distinguished by the white-rimmed dark spot behind the eye band that the Pearlscale shows but C. mertensii does not.

Water parameter cross-source summary: Temperature ranged from 22–29°C across sources (LiveAquaria 22.2–25.6°C, FishBase preferred 25.3–29°C, Tropical Fish Keeping 23.3–26.7°C); the averaged range of 24–27°C is used here. Adult size: LiveAquaria cites 15 cm (6 in) max, FishBase records 14 cm SL maximum, ReefApp 14 cm, Tropical Fish Keeping ~15 cm — range 12–15 cm used here. Tank minimum volume: LiveAquaria 70 gal (~265 L), ReefApp 300 L, Cincinnati Coral 100 gal (~378 L), Tropical Fish Keeping 100 gal (~378 L); biased toward the cautious-larger end and rounded to 300 L. Lifespan: Tropical Fish Keeping reports 'over 7 years' — recorded here as 7–10 years.
breedingNotes: Generally considered not bred in home aquaria. In the wild, has been observed forming monogamous pairs during the breeding season and is presumed to be a broadcast spawner like other Chaetodon, releasing pelagic eggs.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Order Acanthuriformes, Family Chaetodontidae), adult size (14 cm SL max), preferred temperature (25.3–29°C, mean 28.1°C), distribution (Western Pacific: Indonesia and Philippines, north to the Ryukyu Islands), and pair-bonding behavior.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (14 cm), minimum tank volume (300 L), 'docile' temperament, and not-reef-safe status. Used in averaging the tank-size disagreement (range 265–378 L across sources).

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (74–80°F = 23.3–26.7°C), pH (8.1–8.4), specific gravity (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), adult size (5.9 in ≈ 15 cm), care level (easy-to-moderate / intermediate), 100 gal (~378 L) minimum tank, and lifespan ('over 7 years'). Averaged into the final parameter values.
